@graph.description: This experiments automates the deployment of a multipath QUIC topology over a MASQUE proxy where a client has two paths to the intermediary. The two paths are heterogeneous with regards to their bandwidth and latency. End-to-end communication therefore occurs over a slow and a fast path. Via application knowledge, the client informs the proxy of its prioritization preferences for each end-to-end connection. The tunnels either use an unreliable or reliable substrate. In the reliable case, losses are protected against, and tunnels carrying end-to-end connections can be given a higher priority than others. The setup demonstrates how legacy and difficult-to-extend protocols can benefit from modern extensions: the target server does not require any changes as it remains unaware of the novel QUIC-based proxying QoS features.
@graph.name: Blueprint for Multipath MASQUE QoS Measurements

results
@graph.description: Result directory for the client, split between reliable vs. unreliable runs. The output folder contains a boxplot visually showing that reliable MASQUE enables individual flows to be prioritized, while unreliable MASQUE does not provide these capabilities. A separate text file contains further statistics that highlight that difference in the end-to-end completion times per priority level. The raw data of each run is stored as a JSON file, both for the outer connection and the nine inner flows. SSL keylog files allow packet captures at the proxy to be decrypted.

@graph.name: results
@graph.description: Result directory for the proxy, split between reliable vs. unreliable runs. The output folder contains the raw data of each run in the JSON format. The inner, end-to-end connections made by the client are opaque to the proxy, as shown by the individual .sqlog file of the received connection. When packet captures are enabled, a .pcapng file is stored that can be decrypted with the SSL keylog file.

@graph.name: results
@graph.description: Result directory for the server, split between reliable vs. unreliable runs. The output folder contains .sqlog files that correspond to each served end-to-end connection that the proxy is able to prioritize.

@graph.name: valga-install-dependencies
@graph.description: Installs common dependencies, such as iperf for validating the topology, the Rust-based MASQUE Nada implementation, and tshark for capturing packets.

@graph.name: rapla-install-dependencies
@graph.description: Installs common dependencies, such as iperf for validating the topology, the Rust-based MASQUE Nada implementation, and tshark for capturing packets.

@graph.name: tapa-install-dependencies
@graph.description: Installs common dependencies, such as iperf for validating the topology, the Rust-based MASQUE Nada implementation, and tshark for capturing packets.

@graph.name: rapla-topology
@graph.description: Configures the client-side topology by assigning distinct addresses per physical path endpoint. Routing rules ensure each source address uses its intended path. Per-path latency and bandwidth parameters are set (egress only).

@graph.name: tapa-topology
@graph.description: Configures the proxy's multipath network. The proxy is addressable with a single IPv6 address. Egress-only, per-path latency and bandwidth parameters are set to reflect the fast and slow paths to the client, as well as a delay toward the server. The bandwidth is left unconstrained on the proxy-server link.

@graph.name: valga-topology
@graph.description: Configures the server's network by assigning it a single IPv6 address. A route to the proxy is configured alongside its delay property; there is no direct link between client and proxy.

@graph.name: valga-generate-files
@graph.description: Creates nine separate 1 MB files that the client is able to retrieve with an end-to-end HTTP/3 GET request over the proxy. One such request will be made per tunnel. The first flow carries a payload of just 0s, the second of 1s, and so forth, facilitating flow distinction when observing decrypted packet traces.

@graph.name: rapla-run-client
@graph.description: Starts a multipath MASQUE client which sends nine CONNECT-UDP requests to the proxy within a single multipath QUIC connection. These are prioritized differently in groups of high-, medium-, and low-priority flows. A flag controls whether reliability for the tunnel's substrate is enabled. Decryption keys and traces are stored for debugging should the proxy be capturing packets.

@graph.name: rapla-run-client_run00
@graph.description: Sends out the resulting request instantiated through client-specific loop variables.

@graph.name: valga-run-server
@graph.description: Starts a common HTTP/3 Web server able to serve files.

@graph.name: valga-run-server_run00
@graph.description: Restarts the server between iterations to prevent previous runs from affecting the current one. The server is unaware of the client.

@graph.name: tapa-run-proxy
@graph.description: Starts the multipath MASQUE Nada proxy and awaits for incoming connections from the client. Settings control whether secrets and qlog traces are stored alongside packet captures on the client- and server-facing interfaces.

@graph.name: tapa-run-proxy_run00
@graph.description: Restarts the proxy between iterations to prevent previous runs from affecting the current one. Awaits for incoming client requests.

@graph.description: Sends out the resulting request instantiated through client-specific loop variables.
